title: Method of understanding the process of human thinking
abstract: A method of understanding the process of thinking of a person in relation to said person making decisions on a particular subject, the method including the steps of using a geometric figure (10) having designated elements at vertices of the geometric figure (10) to develop a matrix (190, 200) that identifies and establishes meamngfui relationships between said designated elements by the person in making said decisions, said decisions being made in response to a sequential set of questions or required actions, and providing a temporal frame of reference that gives context to the sequence of questions or required actions.
claims:
Claims CLAIMS: 1. A method of understanding the process of thinking of a person in relation to said person making decisions on a particular subject, the method including the steps of: using a geometric figure having designated elements at vertices of the geometric figure to develop a matrix that identifies and establishes meaningful relationships between said designated elements by the person in making said decisions; said decisions being made in response to a sequential set of questions or required actions; providing a temporal frame of reference that gives context to the sequence of questions or required actions, 2. A method according to claim 1 further including providing said set of questions or required actions at vertices of said geometric figure, where each question is associated with a designated element. 3. A method according to claim 2 wherein the geometric figure is a representation of a neural network. 4. A method according to claim 3 wherein the neural network operates on the basis of information transfer using Clathrin neurotransmitters that provide message transmission across the neural network. 5. A method according to any one of claims 2 to 4 further including providing the sequential set of questions or required actions in a three dimensional form of the geometric figure. 6. A method according to any one of the previous claims wherein the matrix includes a number of cells, with each cell having a desigiiated colour to represent a designated element. 7. A method according to claim 6 wherein the designated elements represent choice and change that the person responds to, with each response being stored for later retrieval, analysis and comparison to other respondents. 8. A method according to claim 7 wherein designated elements are aligned in pairs to respectively form a pole in the geometric figure, wherein each pole represents potential 

 technological contribution of each respondent to a particular measure of future contribution identified as habits of mind of the person. 9. A method according to claim 8 wherein the designated elements that form up to six poles include CAN to COULD, IF to IF ONLY/ONLY IF, MAY to SHOULD, MUST to WILL, THEN/WHEN to NOW and WH ICH to HOW. 10. A method according to claim 9 wherein the designated elements of each pole, at each end thereof, represent colour-coded habits of mind and the relative space between each of the paired elements of a pole and between each pole, based on the decisions of the person, dictates the overall three-dimensional shape of the geometric figure as applied to said person. 1 1. A method according to claim 10 wherein said overall shape provides an indication of structural distortions and/or organisation in order to assist in identifying necessary and sufficient decisions about said person. 12. A method according to any one of the previous claims wherein the responses to questions or required actions of the person generate said matrix in two and three dimensions, with the position and size of each coloured cell providing a pattern about the person's personal preferences and patterns of decision making that can be compared for degrees of ali gnment to another individual or an organisation. 13. A method according to claim 12 further including calculating a TQ or technology quadrant score from the matrix which indicates the relative standing of one person against another person. 14. A method according to claim 13 further including applying a TQ score to each of the cells of the matrix which indicates preferences in the decision making of the person. 15. A method according to claim 14 wherein a three dimensional matrix provides a topographical representation of the coloured cells with raised cells representing a high TQ score and low cells representing a neutral response or low TQ score. 16. A matri resulting f rom the method of any one of claims 1 to 15, said matrix having a plurality of cells, with each cell having a designated colour to represent a designated element. 17. A matrix according to claim 16 that is in two or three dimensions, with the position and size of each coloured cell providing a pattern about the person's personal preferences and patterns of decision making that can be compared for degrees of alignment to another individual or an organisation. 18. A matrix according to claim 17 wherein a TQ or technology quadrant score is calculated from the matrix , the TQ score indicating the relative standing of one person against another person. 19. A matrix according to claim 18 wherein a TQ score is applied to each of the cells of the matrix which indicates preferences in the decision making of the person. 20. A matrix according to claim 18 or claim 19 wherein a three dimensional matrix provides a topographical representation of the coloured cells with raised cells representing a high TQ score and low cells representing a neutral response or low TQ score.
